nodes.reasoning: strings /tmp/traffic.pcap | grep "14ce18" or tcpdump -r /tmp/traffic.pcap 'port 80' -A | strings | grep "14ce18" tcpdump - the packet capture tool -r /tmp/traffic.pcap - read from the existing capture file (instead of capturing live) 'port 80' - filter to show only packets on port 80 (HTTP traffic) -A - print packet contents in ASCII format (human-readable text) | strings - pipe output to strings command, which extracts only printable text (filters> | grep "14ce18" - pipe to grep to search for lines containing "14ce18"

actions_effects.action_description: Prose notes describing how to filter a pcap file with tcpdump and grep for a flag string.

nodes.reasoning: Exactly the same as flag cd699a to enter into the tomcat thing thru metasploit but then I need to navigate to the C folder again or just activate powershell In powershell, do the following commands Get-Module -ListAvailable Import-Module ActiveDirectory Get-ADForest Get-ADDomainController Get-ADObject -Filter * -Properties * | Where-Object {$_.Description -match "59ecca"} and then flag is found Topics Covered: 1. PowerShell Basics Using PowerShell command-line interface Running cmdlets (PowerShell commands) Piping output between commands using | 2. PowerShell Module Management Get-Module -ListAvailable: Discovering what modules are installed on the system Import-Module: Loading modules to access their cmdlets (specifically the ActiveDirectory module) 3. Active Directory (AD) Enumeration Domain Information: Used Get-ADDomain to gather details about the AD domain (name, controllers, SID, etc.) Forest Information: Used Get-ADForest to understand the forest structure (root domain, forest functional level) Domain Controller Discovery: Used Get-ADDomainController to identify and gather info about domain controllers (server names, IPs, FSMO roles) 4. Active Directory Object Querying Get-ADObject: Retrieved ALL objects from Active Directory -Filter *: Used wildcard to get every object (users, computers, groups, OUs, etc.) -Properties *: Pulled all properties/attributes for each object 5. Data Filtering with PowerShell Where-Object: Filtered results based on conditions Regular expression matching: Used -match operator to search for patterns Pattern searching: Looked for keywords like "flag", "CTF", and special characters { } commonly found in CTF flags 6. CTF Flag Discovery Searched AD object descriptions for hidden flags Used enumeration techniques to find flags embedded in AD metadata Summary: Windows Active Directory reconnaissance and enumeration using PowerShell to discover domain structure and locate hidden CTF flags in object properties

actions_effects.action_description: Prose notes describing Active Directory enumeration via PowerShell to locate a CTF flag in object descriptions.
